annotation.text:
Cultflicks is a SaaS based streaming platform that became popular for streaming unpopular movies.
It had sold subscriptions for a long time but eventually, the growth stalled.
The company wanted to improve its product offering for casual viewers by adding a pay-per-view subscription model.
This would mean that subscribers would only pay for the content that they watched and if they didn't watch anything, they wouldn't be charged for that month.

After a successful advertising campaign, Cultflicks now has customers across two different subscription levels.
Premium subscribers, who pay a standard monthly fee for all content, and pay-per-view subscribers whose monthly charge will vary depending on their consumption each month.

The Cultflicks example stream will provide a batch flow that records viewing statistics, classifies users according to their usage, and processes data into billable information.
This stream highlights:

An actual stream flow from raw data

Data enrichment

User classification to multiple use cases, like billing, notification, settlement and analytics

A step-by-step preview of data transformation from "raw usage data" to data that the business can gain insight from, for example, data related to churn or upselling opportunities.

This example stream is explained in detail here: https://infozone.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/DAZ/pages/7880549/Cultflicks+Video+Streaming.

mappings.expression: "Hi dear " + @{payload.name} + ", Your email is " + @{payload.email} + ", your country is " + @{payload.country} + ", and you were born on " + @{payload.birth_year} + ". " + @{payload.nudge_msg} + " " + @{payload.metrics_msg} + "  Best regards, Cultflicks"

annotation.text: Calculate the view percent per view event record, based on actual measured view duration (from the usage data), and maximum view duration (based on the product master data we looked up previously), so we can see how much of each movie was actually watched, and enrich them with this percentage. We use a Map processor for this, which has a simple formula language that can be used for simple calculations like this one. We then send the enriched view event records to two different places, both for billing and for a view completion checks.

Split view events into completed or not completed based on the view percent value we calculated in the previous function. We use the Route function and its condition rules for this. Based on this classifiction, we send the completed ones to Settlement, and the not completed ones to Analytics.

Here we use a Script processor to format an email message to users with a friendly message, some usage statistics, and a nudge message, which we then simulate sending using a No-Op forwarder. In a real life scenario, this would use an Email Notification forwarder.

Use Count and Script functions to generate simulated sample usage data representing views of really, really bad movies. In a real world scenario, we would have collected this from video streaming server databases, using one or more e.g. Amazon S3, Azure Blob Storage, Google Cloud Storage, HTTP Client, or SFTP collectors. In a real-world scenario, there would have been a validation step after collection, to ensure data is correct and clean.

Here we prepare usage data summaries for analytics. We use a Data Aggregator processor to aggregate uncompleted view events per movie (not user) per month, in order to be able to send data to an analytics database where Cultflicks can determine how their content is performing. We then format them and simulate sending them to an external system using a No-op forwarder. In a real-world scenario, we may have sent it to an analytics database, using the appropriate type of forwarder, for example a Database forwarder.

Here we prepare usage data summaries for settlement. We use a Data Aggregator processor to aggregate completed view events per movie (not user) per month, in order for a downstream system to calculate how much royalty we should pay to each owner of the movie IP content. We then format them and simulate sending them to an external system using a No-op forwarder. In a real-world scenario, we may have sent it to, for example, an ERP system responsible for accounts payable, or a billing system used for partner settlements, using the appropriate type of forwarder.

Use a Data Aggregator processor to aggregate view events per user per month, then use this aggregated data to classify users: Are they light, medium or heavy users? Is there a risk they would churn? Based on these classifications, we generate some information and nudging messages that would be suitable to email the user and/or include or their next invoice. Here, uwe use a Script aggregator to do some very simplistic rules-based classifications. In a real-world scenario, these classifications would probably have been done by an external call to an AI service using a machine learning model to classify users, for which we would have used e.g. an HTTP Client processor. Finally we format the data for billing, and simulate sending it to a billing system using a No-op forwarder. In a real-world scenario, we would have used e.g. a SAP Subscription Billing forwarder, or a Zuora Usage File forwarder, and an Email forwarder.

Simulate looking up the master data from external systems, then use it to enrich the usage data with user and product information. In this case, the product information is data about the movie, and the user information is, among other things, mapping a technical userId to a user email and other information we will need later in the stream. We use the Script processors for this here. In a real world scenario, we would have used processors like for example Database query, HTTP Client, Salesforce query or SAP Subscription Billing query to do these lookups in external systems. In this simple example, we mock this using the Script functions instead.
